# Optimizer — Findings

*Generated by `pipeline/optimize.py` (OR-Tools CP-SAT). Full data:
`public/data/optimizer.json`, `data/processed/optimized_headways.parquet`.
Every constraint below is asserted in code (`test_optimize.py`), not assumed.*

## Headline

**Calgary's bus network could save ~5.0M vehicle-km per year — about 10%
of bus vehicle-km, ≈2.5M litres of diesel, ≈6,700 t CO₂, ≈$4.1M/yr — with
modeled ridership impact under 2% and no stop losing service in any
period it has today.** (Conservative scenario; demand modeled, not
measured — see FINDINGS-demand.md.)

| scenario | saved km/yr | % of bus vkm | diesel L/yr | t CO₂/yr | $/yr | ridership impact | cells changed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| conservative | 5.03M | 10.4% | 2.51M | 6,739 | $4.1M | −2.0% | 406/1217 |
| moderate | 6.85M | 14.2% | 3.43M | 9,186 | $5.7M | −3.5% | 519/1217 |
| aggressive | 8.02M | 16.6% | 4.01M | 10,743 | $6.6M | −5.0% | 619/1217 |

## What the optimizer is allowed to do

Choose a longer headway per (route, period, day-type) from
{10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 45, 60} min, subject to:

1. **Capacity** — modeled peak load per trip (which concentrates as trips
   thin out) stays within 55 seats × policy load factor (0.8 / 1.0 / 1.25
   by scenario).
2. **Availability** — no stop loses service in any period it has today;
   nothing goes worse than hourly; already-hourly service is untouched;
   periods with <4 trips keep their schedule.
3. **Service standards** — routes now at ≤15 min stay ≤15 min (the
   frequent-network promise, mirroring RouteAhead's Primary Transit
   Network), and no wait more than doubles.
4. **Ridership budget** — boardings lost to longer waits (headway
   elasticity −0.4, linear approximation) stay under a system-wide cap
   (2% / 3.5% / 5% by scenario). CP-SAT picks the most
   fuel-per-lost-rider-efficient set of changes.
5. **Fleet** — headways never shorten, so trips and peak vehicles only
   decrease (asserted).

## Why the guardrails exist (methodological honesty)

The first, capacity-only formulation "saved" 49–55% of vehicle-km by
cutting MAX BRT lines to hourly — a textbook case of an optimizer
exploiting its model's weakest point (the gravity model under-ranks
trunk/BRT demand; see FINDINGS-demand.md limitations). Adding the
frequent-network and doubling guardrails brought it to ~35%, still
implausible: with loads thin almost everywhere, "cut everything to the
policy floor" was the optimum. The ridership-loss budget is what turns
the exercise into a genuine trade-off, and it is the binding constraint
in all three scenarios. The lesson worth stating in any pitch: **the
savings estimate is only as strong as the constraint set**, and this one
is deliberately conservative.

## Where the savings come from

- Overwhelmingly **weekend daytime and weekday off-peak** frequency trims
  on long, thin routes — e.g. route 23 (19→30 min Sat/Sun), route 43
  (21→30), route 302 (26→45), route 20 (27→45).
- The flagged 27 long/low-frequency routes from the supply analysis
  contribute, but so do mid-frequency crosstowns whose modeled loads
  leave headroom even at doubled headways.
- Nothing comes from the frequent core on weekdays — the PTN guardrail
  and capacity constraint hold it in place.

## Caveats

- **Demand is modeled** (calibrated gravity; totals anchored to published
  boardings, spatial distribution unvalidated). The ridership-impact
  figure inherits that uncertainty. APC data would firm both up.
- Savings assume trips scale linearly with headway (span and route
  unchanged) and ignore schedule-blocking efficiencies/losses; a real
  runcut would shift the numbers some percent either way.
- Fuel factor 0.5 L/km is fleet-average diesel; Calgary's CNG/BEB share
  reduces the litres (not the km). Restate before any public claim.
- Elasticity −0.4 is a literature constant, not estimated from Calgary
  data; the scenario spread (2–5% budget) is the sensitivity treatment.
- Coordinated-offset scheduling on overlapping corridors (supply finding
  #3) is not yet modeled — it's upside, not risk.
